chunk_summary: Guidelines for charting preterm infant growth using corrected age, preterm-specific charts, and WHO standards, emphasizing z-scores, growth velocity, and follow-up care.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of corrected age in growth charting for preterm infants.
Learn how to interpret z-scores and growth velocity for preterm infants using WHO/IAP and preterm-specific charts.
Identify appropriate follow-up intervals and nutritional interventions for preterm infants.
Recognize the implications of extrauterine growth restriction (EUGR) and its management.
key_facts: Corrected age is calculated as chronological age minus weeks born before 40 weeks (e.g., a 16-week-old born at 32 weeks is plotted at 8 weeks corrected age).
Use corrected age for growth charting until at least 24 months, extending to 36 months for very preterm infants.
Preterm-specific charts like Fenton are used for 22 to 50 weeks postmenstrual age.
Frequent follow-up is recommended: weekly or fortnightly weights post-discharge, then monthly plotting during infancy.
Persistent decline in weight-for-age z-score or head growth warrants targeted nutrition review and neurodevelopmental surveillance.
WHO term charts are used after discharge, with corrected age applied for accurate plotting.
reference_quote: Corrected age equals chronological age minus the number of weeks born before 40 weeks (for example, a 16-week-old born at 32 weeks is plotted at 8 weeks corrected age).
